preFix.title: Rust remover paste
preFix.subtitle: A citric acid, bicarbonate of soda, and salt paste for rust spots on cast iron, tools, and garden furniture
preFix.excerpt: Citric acid dissolves iron oxide (rust) by chelating the iron ions from the surface. Combined with bicarbonate of soda and salt into a paste, it stays in contact with the rust long enough to work. Rinse and dry the metal immediately after use to prevent re-rusting.
preFix.sourceNotes: Citric acid rust removal chemistry from standard chemistry references and cast iron restoration guides.

content.text: binds to the iron ions in the rust and lifts them from the metal surface. Bicarbonate of soda as a paste binder slows the reaction slightly and makes the paste easier to apply and keep in contact with the surface. Dry the metal thoroughly after treatment to prevent
; for cast iron, apply a very thin layer of vegetable oil immediately after drying.

items.name: Water
items.prepNote: A few drops to form a thick paste

content.text: Combine citric acid, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in the bowl. Add water a few drops at a time and stir until a thick paste forms. The mixture will fizz slightly as the citric acid reacts with the bicarbonate; this is normal. Apply the paste to rust spots and leave for 30 to 60 minutes, keeping it moist by covering with cling film if needed.

Scrub with a stiff brush. Rinse thoroughly under running water. Dry immediately and completely with a clean cloth. For cast iron, heat briefly in a low oven to ensure all moisture is gone, then apply a very thin coat of vegetable oil while still warm.

Mix fresh for each use. Discard unused paste. The dry ingredients (citric acid, bicarbonate of soda, salt) can be pre-combined in a jar and stored for up to 12 months; add water only at the point of use.

preFix.yieldDescription: Small batch for one cleaning session
